title: Check if String Has All Unique Characters
description: Return YES if all characters are unique, else NO.

examples.explanation: String length n=5. Outer loop i=0 (char 'a'): Inner loop j=1 to 4: compare 'a' with 'b','c','d','e' → all different. i=1 (char 'b'): j=2 to 4: compare 'b' with 'c','d','e' → all different. i=2 (char 'c'): j=3 to 4: compare 'c' with 'd','e' → all different. i=3 (char 'd'): j=4: compare 'd' with 'e' → different. Loop ends (i stops at 3 since n-1=4). No duplicates found → return "YES".

String length n=5. Outer loop i=0 (char 'h'): Inner loop j=1 to 4: compare 'h' with 'e','l','l','o' → all different. i=1 (char 'e'): j=2 to 4: compare 'e' with 'l','l','o' → all different. i=2 (char 'l'): j=3: compare 'l' with 'l' → MATCH found at i=2, j=3. Immediately return "NO" without completing remaining loops.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Compare each character with every other character in the string using nested loops. If any duplicate is found, return NO; otherwise return YES after all comparisons.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Unique Character Check
ladders.desc: Complete implementation checking if all characters in a string are unique using nested loops.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the problem by comparing each character with every other character in the string. The intuition is straightforward: if any character appears more than once, the string fails the uniqueness test. We use two nested loops: the outer loop selects a character as reference, and the inner loop compares it with all subsequent characters. This ensures every possible pair is checked exactly once. The algorithm starts by iterating through each character at index i from 0 to n-2 (since the last character has no subsequent characters to compare). For each i, we iterate through characters at index j from i+1 to n-1. If string[i] equals string[j], we immediately return "NO" because a duplicate is found. If all comparisons complete without finding duplicates, we return "YES". Time complexity is O(n²) due to nested loops, where n is string length. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete nested loop structure. Common mistakes include: comparing characters with themselves (avoid by starting j at i+1), not handling empty strings (should return "YES"), and inefficient early termination (we do terminate early on duplicate). Real code pattern: def has_unique_chars(s): n = len(s); for i in range(n-1): for j in range(i+1, n): if s[i] == s[j]: return "NO"; return "YES".

examples.trace: String length n=5. Outer loop i=0 (char 'a'): Inner loop j=1 to 4: compare 'a' with 'b','c','d','e' → all different. i=1 (char 'b'): j=2 to 4: compare 'b' with 'c','d','e' → all different. i=2 (char 'c'): j=3 to 4: compare 'c' with 'd','e' → all different. i=3 (char 'd'): j=4: compare 'd' with 'e' → different. Loop ends (i stops at 3 since n-1=4). No duplicates found → return "YES".

String length n=5. Outer loop i=0 (char 'h'): Inner loop j=1 to 4: compare 'h' with 'e','l','l','o' → all different. i=1 (char 'e'): j=2 to 4: compare 'e' with 'l','l','o' → all different. i=2 (char 'l'): j=3: compare 'l' with 'l' → MATCH found at i=2, j=3. Immediately return "NO" without completing remaining loops.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Nested Loop Pair Comparison
ladders.desc: Implement nested loops to compare each character with all subsequent characters.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine forms the core of the brute force approach by systematically comparing every character pair in the string. The intuition is that to check uniqueness, we need to examine all possible (i,j) pairs where i<j. We achieve this using two loops: an outer loop iterates through each character position i from 0 to n-2, and an inner loop iterates through positions j from i+1 to n-1. This ensures each pair is checked exactly once without redundancy. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Get string length n. 2. For i from 0 to n-2: 3. For j from i+1 to n-1: 4. Compare characters at positions i and j. 5. If equal, we've found a duplicate (but in this sub-routine we just identify it; L0 handles returning the result). Time complexity is O(n²) as we have nested loops over n elements. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 by providing the comparison engine; L0 adds the return logic. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in loop ranges (remember j starts at i+1, not i), and not considering that the last character (i=n-1) doesn't need an inner loop. Real code pattern: n = len(s); for i in range(n-1): for j in range(i+1, n): if s[i] == s[j]: duplicate_found = True.

examples.trace: String length n=3. Outer loop i=0 (char 'a'): Inner loop j=1 to 2: compare 'a' with 'b' (different), 'a' with 'c' (different). i=1 (char 'b'): Inner loop j=2: compare 'b' with 'c' (different). Loop ends (i stops at 1 since n-1=2). All comparisons complete with no matches.

String length n=3. Outer loop i=0 (char 'a'): Inner loop j=1 to 2: compare 'a' with 'b' (different), 'a' with 'a' (MATCH at i=0, j=2). Duplicate identified. Note: We don't check i=1 because we found duplicate early (in L0 we'd return immediately).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Pairwise Character Comparison
ladders.desc: Compare two characters at different positions to check if they are equal.
ladders.explanation: This core logic is the fundamental operation that determines if two characters are duplicates. The intuition is simple: in a string, characters are duplicates if they are identical when compared at different indices. The algorithm involves: given a string s and two indices i and j (with i ≠ j), access s[i] and s[j], then check if they are equal using the equality operator (==). If equal, the characters are duplicates; if not, they are unique. Time complexity is O(1) for a single comparison. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 by being the operation performed inside the nested loops; L1 extends this to all pairs. Common mistakes include: comparing characters with the same index (i=j, which always matches but isn't a real duplicate), and not handling case sensitivity (in this problem, we assume exact character matching). Real code pattern: if s[i] == s[j]: return True (for duplicate).

examples.trace: String s = "hello". Access s[1] = 'e'. Access s[3] = 'l'. Compare 'e' == 'l' → False. Characters are different.
String s = "hello". Access s[2] = 'l'. Access s[3] = 'l'. Compare 'l' == 'l' → True. Characters are duplicates.

testCases.expected: True (but indices invalid for empty string)

ladders.title: Building Block — String Indexing and Iteration
ladders.desc: Access characters in a string by index and iterate through them using loops.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves two key skills: accessing individual characters in a string using indices, and looping through all characters. The intuition is that strings are sequences where each character has a position (index) starting from 0. To check uniqueness, we need to examine each character, which requires iterating through the string. The algorithm: 1. Understand string indexing: for a string s of length n, valid indices are 0 to n-1. Access s[i] gets the character at position i. 2. Use a for-loop to iterate: for i in range(len(s)): process s[i]. Time complexity for iterating once is O(n). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 by providing the mechanism to get characters for comparison; L2 uses these accessed characters. Common mistakes include: index out of bounds (accessing beyond n-1), and confusion between zero-based and one-based indexing. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(s)): char = s[i].

examples.trace: String s = "hello", length n=5. Loop i from 0 to 4: i=0 → s[0]='h'; i=1 → s[1]='e'; i=2 → s[2]='l'; i=3 → s[3]='l'; i=4 → s[4]='o'. Each character accessed sequentially.
String s = "ab", length n=2. Loop i from 0 to 1: i=0 → s[0]='a'; i=1 → s[1]='b'. Simple two-step iteration.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Equality Check
ladders.desc: Compare two single characters to see if they are the same.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is the simplest form of comparison: given two characters, check if they are identical. The intuition is that characters are primitive data types (like letters or digits) that can be compared directly using the equality operator. In programming, characters are often represented as strings of length 1 or char types. The algorithm: take two characters, char1 and char2, and evaluate the expression char1 == char2. If true, they are the same; if false, they are different. Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 by being the comparison used when we access characters from string indices; L3 extends this to accessing those characters first. Common mistakes include: confusing assignment (=) with comparison (==), and not considering case sensitivity (e.g., 'A' != 'a'). Real code pattern: if char1 == char2: result = True else: result = False.

examples.trace: Compare 'a' == 'b'. Evaluate: characters 'a' and 'b' are different → result is False.
Compare 'x' == 'x'. Evaluate: both characters are 'x' → result is True.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Strings as Character Sequences
ladders.desc: Recognize that a string is an ordered collection of characters.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces the basic idea of a string in programming. The intuition is that a string is not just a single entity but a sequence of individual characters arranged in a specific order. For example, the string "cat" consists of characters 'c', 'a', and 't' in that sequence. Understanding this is crucial because checking for unique characters requires examining each character in the sequence. Key points: 1. A string has a length (number of characters). 2. Characters can be letters, digits, symbols, or spaces. 3. The order matters ("ab" is different from "ba"). 4. Strings can be empty (length 0). This connects to L4 by providing the characters that will be compared; L4 operates on these characters. Common mistakes include: treating a string as a single object without considering its components, and not realizing that spaces or punctuation are also characters. Real code pattern: s = "example" (creates a string with characters 'e','x','a','m','p','l','e').

examples.trace: String "dog" has length 3. It is composed of three characters: position 0 is 'd', position 1 is 'o', position 2 is 'g'. These are in order: first 'd', then 'o', then 'g'.
String "42" has length 2. It is composed of two characters: position 0 is '4', position 1 is '2'. Even though it looks like a number, it's a string with character '4' followed by '2'.

approaches.name: Hash Set O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a hash set to track seen characters while iterating through the string. If a character is already in the set, return NO; otherwise add it to the set and continue.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(min(n, c)) where c is character set size

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check String for All Unique Characters Using Hash Set
ladders.desc: Complete solution using hash set to detect duplicate characters in a string.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of checking if all characters in a string are unique using a hash set approach. The intuition is that a hash set provides O(1) average time complexity for insertion and lookup operations, allowing us to efficiently track which characters we've already encountered. As we iterate through each character in the string, we check if it exists in our set. If it does, we've found a duplicate and can immediately return NO. If not, we add it to the set and continue. This approach works because sets enforce uniqueness - they cannot contain duplicate elements. The algorithm terminates early upon finding the first duplicate, which optimizes for strings with early duplicates. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the string length, as we potentially examine each character once. Space complexity is O(min(n, c)) where c is the character set size (e.g., 128 for ASCII, 256 for extended ASCII, or more for Unicode), since the set can grow up to the number of unique characters in the string, but is bounded by the character set size. Connection to L1: This full solution calls the character tracking subroutine from L1 within the main iteration loop. Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling empty strings (should return YES), 2) Case sensitivity issues (depends on problem specification), 3) Forgetting to convert characters to a consistent case if needed, 4) Using inefficient data structures like lists for lookups (O(n) vs O(1)), 5) Not considering Unicode characters properly. Real code pattern: def has_all_unique_chars(s): seen = set(); for char in s: if char in seen: return 'NO'; seen.add(char); return 'YES'

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty set seen = {}. Step 2: Iterate char 'a': 'a' not in seen → add 'a' → seen = {'a'}. Step 3: Iterate char 'b': 'b' not in seen → add 'b' → seen = {'a', 'b'}. Step 4: Iterate char 'c': 'c' not in seen → add 'c' → seen = {'a', 'b', 'c'}. Step 5: Iterate char 'd': 'd' not in seen → add 'd' → seen = {'a', 'b', 'c', 'd'}. Step 6: Iterate char 'e': 'e' not in seen → add 'e' → seen = {'a', 'b', 'c', 'd', 'e'}. Step 7: End of string, no duplicates found → return 'YES'.

Step 1: Initialize empty set seen = {}. Step 2: Iterate char 'h': 'h' not in seen → add 'h' → seen = {'h'}. Step 3: Iterate char 'e': 'e' not in seen → add 'e' → seen = {'h', 'e'}. Step 4: Iterate char 'l': 'l' not in seen → add 'l' → seen = {'h', 'e', 'l'}. Step 5: Iterate char 'l': 'l' IS in seen → duplicate found → immediately return 'NO'. Algorithm terminates early without checking remaining characters.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Track Seen Characters in Hash Set
ladders.desc: Implement the character tracking mechanism using a hash set.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the core tracking mechanism: maintaining a collection of characters we've already seen and checking if new characters are duplicates. The intuition is that we need a data structure that supports fast insertion and membership testing. A hash set is ideal because it provides average O(1) time for both add() and in operations. The algorithm works by: 1) Initializing an empty set, 2) For each character, checking if it exists in the set, 3) If it does, we've found a duplicate (return False), 4) If not, add it to the set and continue. This sub-problem isolates the tracking logic from the full string iteration. Time complexity for checking one character is O(1) average case for the hash set operations. Space complexity grows with the number of unique characters added, up to O(min(n, c)). Connection to L0: This is the inner loop logic that gets called for each character in the full solution. Connection to L2: This implements the duplicate detection technique using hash sets. Common mistakes: 1) Using a list instead of a set (makes membership testing O(n)), 2) Not handling the case where the character is already in the set properly, 3) Forgetting to initialize the set, 4) Adding characters before checking for duplicates (would always return False). Real code pattern: def track_characters(char, seen): if char in seen: return False; seen.add(char); return True

examples.trace: Step 1: Input char = 'a', seen = {'b', 'c'}. Step 2: Check if 'a' in seen → 'a' not in {'b', 'c'} → condition False. Step 3: Add 'a' to seen → seen becomes {'b', 'c', 'a'}. Step 4: Return True (no duplicate found, character tracked successfully).

Step 1: Input char = 'b', seen = {'a', 'b', 'c'}. Step 2: Check if 'b' in seen → 'b' IS in {'a', 'b', 'c'} → condition True. Step 3: Immediately return False (duplicate found). Set remains unchanged as {'a', 'b', 'c'}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Duplicate Detection Using Hash Sets
ladders.desc: Understand how hash sets enable efficient duplicate detection.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the central insight: using a hash set's properties to detect duplicates efficiently. The intuition is that hash sets store unique elements and provide O(1) average time complexity for checking if an element exists (membership test). When we encounter a character, we check if it's already in our set. If yes, it's a duplicate. If no, we add it. This works because sets use hashing to distribute elements, allowing constant-time operations on average. The key insight is that we're trading space for time - we use extra memory to store seen characters, but gain the ability to detect duplicates in linear time rather than the O(n²) approach of comparing each character with every other character. The algorithm: 1) Create empty hash set, 2) For each new element, check set membership, 3) If present → duplicate found, 4) If not present → add to set. Time complexity per operation is O(1) average, O(n) worst-case if all elements hash to same bucket (unlikely with good hash function). Space is O(k) where k is number of unique elements. Connection to L1: This is the conceptual foundation for the tracking subroutine. Connection to L3: This builds upon understanding hash set operations. Common mistakes: 1) Not understanding that sets don't maintain insertion order, 2) Confusing sets with lists or arrays, 3) Not considering hash collisions, 4) Assuming O(1) is guaranteed (it's average case). Real code pattern: Concept: Use set() for O(1) lookups to find duplicates.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize set = {}. Step 2: Process 1: 1 not in set → add 1 → set = {1}. Step 3: Process 2: 2 not in set → add 2 → set = {1, 2}. Step 4: Process 3: 3 not in set → add 3 → set = {1, 2, 3}. Step 5: Process 2: 2 IS in set {1, 2, 3} → duplicate found. Step 6: Return 'duplicate found' without processing remaining elements.

Step 1: Initialize set = {}. Step 2: Process 'a': 'a' not in set → add 'a' → set = {'a'}. Step 3: Process 'b': 'b' not in set → add 'b' → set = {'a', 'b'}. Step 4: Process 'c': 'c' not in set → add 'c' → set = {'a', 'b', 'c'}. Step 5: Process 'd': 'd' not in set → add 'd' → set = {'a', 'b', 'c', 'd'}. Step 6: End of sequence → return 'no duplicates found'.

ladders.title: Building Block — Hash Set Operations: Add and Membership Test
ladders.desc: Master the fundamental hash set operations needed for duplicate detection.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the two key hash set operations: adding elements and checking membership. The intuition is that hash sets are built on hash tables, which map keys to indices using a hash function. When we add an element: 1) Compute its hash code, 2) Map to a bucket/index, 3) Store the element if not already present (ensuring uniqueness). When we check membership: 1) Compute hash code, 2) Map to bucket, 3) Check if element exists in that bucket. Both operations average O(1) time because the hash function distributes elements evenly across buckets. The uniqueness property is crucial - sets automatically prevent duplicates when adding. The algorithm for our use case: Use add() to insert new characters and the 'in' operator to check existence. Time complexity: O(1) average for both operations. Space: O(n) for storing n unique elements. Connection to L2: These operations enable the duplicate detection logic. Connection to L4: These build upon basic collection manipulation. Common mistakes: 1) Confusing set.add() with list.append(), 2) Using wrong syntax for membership test, 3) Not realizing sets are unordered, 4) Assuming sets allow duplicate elements, 5) Forgetting that sets require hashable elements. Real code pattern: my_set = set(); my_set.add(element); if element in my_set: ...

examples.input: Operations on empty set: add('a'), check 'a' in set, add('b'), check 'c' in set
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize set = {}. Step 2: add('a') → set becomes {'a'}. Step 3: Check 'a' in set → 'a' is in {'a'} → returns True. Step 4: add('b') → set becomes {'a', 'b'}. Step 5: Check 'c' in set → 'c' is not in {'a', 'b'} → returns False.

examples.input: Operations: add('x'), add('x'), check 'x' in set, check 'y' in set
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize set = {}. Step 2: add('x') → set becomes {'x'}. Step 3: add('x') again → set remains {'x'} (sets ignore duplicates). Step 4: Check 'x' in set → 'x' is in {'x'} → returns True. Step 5: Check 'y' in set → 'y' is not in {'x'} → returns False.

testCases.input: add('a') to empty set, then check 'a' membership
add('1') to empty set, add('2'), check '1' membership

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Iteration and Character Access
ladders.desc: Learn to iterate through strings and access individual characters.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental skill of traversing a string character by character, which is essential for the hash set approach. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we need to examine each one to check for duplicates. In most programming languages, strings are iterable, meaning we can loop through them directly. The basic algorithm: Use a loop (for or while) to go through each character position from first to last. For each iteration, access the current character using indexing or iteration variable. Time complexity is O(n) for visiting n characters. Space complexity is O(1) if we just access characters without storing them (though we'll store them in the hash set in the full solution). Connection to L3: This iteration provides the characters that we'll add to and check in the hash set. Connection to L5: This builds upon understanding strings as collections. Common mistakes: 1) Using wrong loop bounds (off-by-one errors), 2) Confusing character with index position, 3) Not handling empty strings, 4) Modifying strings while iterating (usually not allowed), 5) Assuming strings are mutable (they're usually immutable). Real code pattern: for char in string: # process char; OR for i in range(len(string)): char = string[i]

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize loop, position 0. Step 2: Access character at index 0 → 'c'. Step 3: Move to next position, index 1. Step 4: Access character at index 1 → 'a'. Step 5: Move to next position, index 2. Step 6: Access character at index 2 → 't'. Step 7: End of string (index 3 is out of bounds) → loop terminates.

Step 1: Initialize loop, position 0. Step 2: Access character at index 0 → 'a'. Step 3: Move to next position, index 1. Step 4: Access character at index 1 → 'b'. Step 5: End of string (index 2 is out of bounds) → loop terminates.

testCases.expected: Characters: h, e, l, l, o

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Strings as Collections of Characters
ladders.desc: Understand that strings are sequences of individual characters.
ladders.explanation: This is the most fundamental concept: understanding what a string is. A string is a data type that represents text as a sequence of characters. Each character has a position (index) starting from 0. For example, in string "hello", 'h' is at index 0, 'e' at index 1, etc. Strings are typically immutable (cannot be changed after creation) in many languages. The length of a string is the number of characters it contains. Empty string has length 0. Characters can be letters, digits, spaces, punctuation, etc. This concept is prerequisite because our algorithm needs to examine each character individually. The intuition: To check for duplicate characters, we first need to understand that strings are made of individual elements we can inspect. Connection to L4: This understanding enables string iteration. Common beginner mistakes: 1) Thinking strings are single entities rather than collections, 2) Confusing string length with memory size, 3) Not knowing how to access individual characters, 4) Assuming all characters are letters, 5) Not understanding zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: string = "text"; first_char = string[0]; length = len(string)

examples.output: 5 characters: a, p, p, l, e at indices 0-4
examples.trace: Step 1: The string "apple" contains 5 characters. Step 2: Index 0: character 'a'. Step 3: Index 1: character 'p'. Step 4: Index 2: character 'p'. Step 5: Index 3: character 'l'. Step 6: Index 4: character 'e'. Step 7: There is no index 5 (out of bounds).

Step 1: The empty string "" has length 0. Step 2: There are no characters. Step 3: There are no valid indices (index 0 is out of bounds). Step 4: Any attempt to access a character would result in an error.

approaches.name: Boolean Array O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a fixed-size boolean array (size 128 for ASCII or 256 for extended ASCII) to mark seen characters. If a character's index is already true, return NO; otherwise mark it true.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check String for All Unique Characters Using Boolean Array
ladders.desc: Complete solution using a fixed-size boolean array to track seen characters.
ladders.explanation: This problem asks us to determine if all characters in a given string are unique. The Boolean Array approach provides an efficient O(n) solution with O(1) space by leveraging the fact that characters can be mapped to array indices. The intuition is simple: we create a fixed-size boolean array (typically 128 for standard ASCII or 256 for extended ASCII) where each index represents a character's ASCII value. As we iterate through the string, we check if the character's corresponding index in the array is already marked as seen (true). If it is, we've found a duplicate and return 'NO'. Otherwise, we mark that index as true and continue. If we complete the iteration without finding duplicates, we return 'YES'. This works because characters are essentially integers (their ASCII codes), allowing direct indexing. The fixed array size gives us constant space regardless of input size. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Create a boolean array 'seen' of size 128 (or 256) initialized to false. 2. For each character 'ch' in the input string: a. Convert 'ch' to its ASCII integer value 'idx'. b. If seen[idx] is true, return 'NO'. c. Otherwise, set seen[idx] = true. 3. After the loop, return 'YES'. Time complexity is O(n) where n is string length, as we process each character once. Space complexity is O(1) because the array size is fixed (128 or 256), independent of input. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete check using the array marking subroutine. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle empty strings (should return 'YES'), using wrong array size (e.g., 26 for only letters), not converting characters to ASCII values properly, or off-by-one errors in array bounds. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def has_unique_chars(s): seen = [False] * 128; for ch in s: idx = ord(ch); if seen[idx]: return 'NO'; seen[idx] = True; return 'YES'.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize seen array of size 128 with all False. Step 2: Process 'a': ASCII value 97, seen[97] is False, set seen[97] = True. Step 3: Process 'b': ASCII 98, seen[98] is False, set seen[98] = True. Step 4: Process 'c': ASCII 99, seen[99] is False, set seen[99] = True. Step 5: Process 'd': ASCII 100, seen[100] is False, set seen[100] = True. Step 6: Process 'e': ASCII 101, seen[101] is False, set seen[101] = True. Step 7: Loop ends, no duplicates found, return 'YES'.

Step 1: Initialize seen array of size 128 with all False. Step 2: Process 'h': ASCII 104, seen[104] is False, set seen[104] = True. Step 3: Process 'e': ASCII 101, seen[101] is False, set seen[101] = True. Step 4: Process 'l': ASCII 108, seen[108] is False, set seen[108] = True. Step 5: Process 'l': ASCII 108, seen[108] is True, duplicate found, return 'NO'.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Mark and Check Character in Boolean Array
ladders.desc: Implement the core function that marks a character as seen and checks for duplicates.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the Boolean Array approach. It focuses on the operation of checking if a character has been seen before and marking it as seen if not. The intuition is that we need a way to track which characters have appeared in the string so far, and a boolean array provides a direct, O(1) access method. Given a character and a boolean array, we convert the character to its ASCII integer index. If the array at that index is true, it means the character has been seen before, indicating a duplicate. Otherwise, we set it to true to mark it as seen. This sub-routine is called repeatedly for each character in the string in the full problem. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Input: character 'ch', boolean array 'seen'. 2. Compute index 'idx' = ASCII value of 'ch'. 3. If seen[idx] is true, return False (duplicate found). 4. Else, set seen[idx] = true and return True (no duplicate). Time complexity is O(1) per call, as it involves constant-time operations (character conversion and array access). Space complexity is O(1) for the operation itself, though the array is passed in. This connects to L0 by being the inner loop that processes each character. Common mistakes include: not handling non-ASCII characters if array size is too small, incorrect ASCII conversion (e.g., using int(ch) without ord in Python), or returning the wrong boolean values. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def check_and_mark(ch, seen): idx = ord(ch); if seen[idx]: return False; seen[idx] = True; return True.

examples.input: Character: 'a', seen array initially all False
examples.trace: Step 1: Input ch='a', seen array of size 128 all False. Step 2: Compute idx = ord('a') = 97. Step 3: Check seen[97] = False. Step 4: Set seen[97] = True. Step 5: Return True (no duplicate).

examples.input: Character: 'a', seen array with seen[97] = True
examples.trace: Step 1: Input ch='a', seen array with seen[97] = True. Step 2: Compute idx = ord('a') = 97. Step 3: Check seen[97] = True. Step 4: Return False (duplicate found).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Using Array Indexing to Track Character Occurrences
ladders.desc: Understand how to use a fixed-size array to map characters to indices for duplicate detection.
ladders.explanation: This level introduces the core insight of the Boolean Array approach: using an array where each index corresponds to a possible character, and the value at that index indicates whether the character has been seen. The intuition is based on direct addressing—since characters have numeric ASCII codes (e.g., 'a' is 97, 'A' is 65), we can use these codes as indices into an array. By setting array[index] to true when we encounter the character, we create a memory of seen characters. To check for duplicates, we simply look up array[index] before setting it. This is efficient because array access is O(1). The key is that the array size must cover all possible characters in the input (e.g., 128 for standard ASCII). The step-by-step logic: 1. Choose array size (e.g., 128 for ASCII). 2. Initialize array with all false. 3. For a character, compute its ASCII index. 4. Use the array to check and update seen status. Time complexity for checking one character is O(1), and space is O(1) for the fixed array. This connects to L1 by providing the mechanism for the check-and-mark operation. Common mistakes include: misunderstanding ASCII vs. Unicode (this approach works for ASCII only), picking wrong array size, or not initializing the array properly. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: seen = [False] * 128; idx = ord(ch); if seen[idx]: # duplicate; else: seen[idx] = True.

examples.input: Character 'c' in an empty tracking array
examples.trace: Step 1: Array size 128, all False. Step 2: Character 'c' has ASCII value 99. Step 3: Check array[99] = False. Step 4: Set array[99] = True to mark 'c' as seen.

examples.input: Character 'c' when index 99 is already True
examples.trace: Step 1: Array with array[99] = True. Step 2: Character 'c' has ASCII value 99. Step 3: Check array[99] = True. Step 4: Identify duplicate since it's already seen.

ladders.title: Building Block — Convert Character to ASCII Index
ladders.desc: Learn how to map a character to its corresponding integer index for array access.
ladders.explanation: This building block is essential for the Boolean Array approach: converting a character to a numeric index that can be used to access an array. The intuition is that in programming, characters are internally represented as numbers (e.g., in ASCII encoding, 'a' is 97, 'b' is 98, etc.). By converting a character to its ASCII code, we get an integer that serves as a perfect index into a fixed-size array. This allows us to use the array as a direct lookup table for character occurrences. The step-by-step process: 1. Take a character as input (e.g., 'x'). 2. Use a function like ord() in Python or casting in other languages to get its ASCII value. 3. The result is an integer between 0 and 127 for standard ASCII (or 0-255 for extended ASCII). This index can then be used to access an array element. Time complexity is O(1), as character conversion is a constant-time operation. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 by providing the index needed for array tracking. Common mistakes include: assuming all characters are letters (need to handle digits, symbols, spaces), forgetting that ASCII values might exceed array bounds if size is too small, or using incorrect conversion functions. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: idx = ord(ch)  # e.g., ord('A') returns 65.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input character is 'a'. Step 2: Use ord('a') function. Step 3: Output integer 97, which is the ASCII value of 'a'.
Step 1: Input character is '0'. Step 2: Use ord('0') function. Step 3: Output integer 48, which is the ASCII value of '0'.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access and Modify Boolean Array Element
ladders.desc: Practice accessing and updating a specific index in a boolean array.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves working with a boolean array: accessing an element at a given index to check its value, and modifying it by setting it to true or false. The intuition is that arrays provide fast, O(1) access to elements using indices. In the context of tracking characters, we use a boolean array where each element represents whether a character (corresponding to that index) has been seen. To check for duplicates, we access the array at the character's index; if it's true, the character is a duplicate. To mark a character as seen, we set that index to true. This operation is fundamental because it enables the efficient tracking mechanism. The step-by-step process: 1. Have a boolean array (e.g., of size 128) initialized to false. 2. Given an index i (an integer), access array[i] to get its current value (true or false). 3. To modify, set array[i] to a new boolean value (e.g., true). Time complexity is O(1) for both access and modification. Space complexity is O(1) per operation. This connects to L3 by using the index obtained from character conversion to interact with the array. Common mistakes include: using wrong index (off-by-one), not initializing the array, or misunderstanding boolean values (true vs. false). Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: arr = [False] * size; value = arr[index]; arr[index] = True.

examples.input: Array of size 5 all False, index 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Array = [False, False, False, False, False]. Step 2: Access arr[2] = False. Step 3: Set arr[2] = True. Step 4: New array = [False, False, True, False, False].
Step 1: Array = [False, False, False, True, False]. Step 2: Access arr[3] = True. Step 3: No modification needed in this access-only example.

testCases.input: arr all False, idx=4, set to True

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Boolean Values and Arrays
ladders.desc: Learn the basics of boolean data type and array data structure.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level covers two key concepts: boolean values and arrays. Booleans are a data type that can have one of two values: true or false (in Python, True or False). They are used to represent logical states, such as whether something has occurred or not. In our problem, we use booleans to track if a character has been seen (true if seen, false if not). Arrays are data structures that store a collection of elements, each accessible by an index (a numeric position). For example, an array of size 5 can hold 5 elements, and you can access the first element with index 0, the second with index 1, etc. In our approach, we use a boolean array, meaning each element is a boolean value. The intuition is that by combining these concepts, we can create a simple lookup system: each array index corresponds to a character, and the boolean at that index tells us if that character has appeared. This is beginner-friendly because it builds on basic programming concepts. Step-by-step: 1. Boolean values: Understand that True means 'yes' or 'has happened', False means 'no' or 'has not happened'. 2. Arrays: Learn how to declare an array (e.g., in Python: arr = [False, False, True]), access an element (e.g., arr[0] gives the first element), and modify it (e.g., arr[1] = True). Time and space are not analyzed here as this is conceptual. This connects to L4 by providing the building blocks for array operations. Common mistakes include: confusing array indices (starting at 0), mixing up boolean values with other types, or not initializing arrays properly. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: is_seen = True; arr = [False, True, False]; value = arr[1]  # returns True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Boolean True indicates something is true or has occurred. Step 2: In context, if a character is seen, we set a boolean to True. Step 3: Example: has_seen_a = True means character 'a' has been seen.
Step 1: Array has three elements: index 0 = False, index 1 = True, index 2 = False. Step 2: To access element at index 1, use arr[1]. Step 3: arr[1] returns True.

testCases.input: Array [True, True, False], modify index 2 to True

approaches.name: Bit Manipulation O(n)
approaches.summary: Use an integer as a bit vector where each bit represents whether a character has been seen. For each character, check and set the corresponding bit using bitwise operations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Check String for Unique Characters Using Bit Manipulation
ladders.desc: Complete solution using bit vector approach to detect duplicate characters in O(n) time and O(1) space.
ladders.explanation: This solution leverages the fact that characters can be represented as bits in an integer, creating an efficient constant-space solution. The intuition is that we can map each character to a specific bit position (0-25 for lowercase letters, 0-51 for both cases, or 0-127 for ASCII). As we iterate through the string, we check if the corresponding bit is already set in our bitmask. If it is, we've found a duplicate. If not, we set that bit and continue. This approach works because each bit acts as a boolean flag for whether we've seen that character before. The algorithm: 1) Initialize bitmask = 0. 2) For each character in string: a) Calculate bit position = ord(char) - ord('a') for lowercase. b) Create mask = 1 << position. c) If (bitmask & mask) != 0: return 'NO' (duplicate found). d) Set bit: bitmask |= mask. 3) Return 'YES' if loop completes. Time complexity is O(n) where n is string length. Space complexity is O(1) since we use a single integer. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete checkBitAndSet function within the full problem context. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle uppercase letters (need to map 'A'-'Z' to 26-51), not resetting bitmask between tests, and incorrect bit shifting. The real pattern: def has_unique_chars(s): bitmask = 0; for ch in s: pos = ord(ch.lower()) - ord('a'); mask = 1 << pos; if bitmask & mask: return 'NO'; bitmask |= mask; return 'YES'.

examples.trace:
String: 'abcde'
Initialize bitmask = 0 (binary: 0)
Character 'a': pos = 0, mask = 1 (binary: 1)
Check: bitmask & mask = 0 & 1 = 0 → no duplicate
Set: bitmask = 0 | 1 = 1 (binary: 1)
Character 'b': pos = 1, mask = 2 (binary: 10)
Check: 1 & 2 = 0 → no duplicate
Set: bitmask = 1 | 2 = 3 (binary: 11)
Character 'c': pos = 2, mask = 4 (binary: 100)
Check: 3 & 4 = 0 → no duplicate
Set: bitmask = 3 | 4 = 7 (binary: 111)
Character 'd': pos = 3, mask = 8 (binary: 1000)
Check: 7 & 8 = 0 → no duplicate
Set: bitmask = 7 | 8 = 15 (binary: 1111)
Character 'e': pos = 4, mask = 16 (binary: 10000)
Check: 15 & 16 = 0 → no duplicate
Set: bitmask = 15 | 16 = 31 (binary: 11111)
Loop complete, return 'YES'

String: 'hello'
Initialize bitmask = 0 (binary: 0)
Character 'h': pos = 7, mask = 128 (binary: 10000000)
Check: 0 & 128 = 0 → no duplicate
Set: bitmask = 0 | 128 = 128 (binary: 10000000)
Character 'e': pos = 4, mask = 16 (binary: 10000)
Check: 128 & 16 = 0 → no duplicate
Set: bitmask = 128 | 16 = 144 (binary: 10010000)
Character 'l': pos = 11, mask = 2048 (binary: 100000000000)
Check: 144 & 2048 = 0 → no duplicate
Set: bitmask = 144 | 2048 = 2192 (binary: 100010010000)
Character 'l': pos = 11, mask = 2048 (binary: 100000000000)
Check: 2192 & 2048 = 2048 (non-zero) → DUPLICATE FOUND
Return 'NO' immediately

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check and Set Bit for Character Detection
ladders.desc: Implement function that checks if character's bit is set and sets it if not.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the bit manipulation approach. The intuition is that we need to atomically check whether we've seen a character before and mark it as seen if we haven't. This prevents race conditions in the algorithm and ensures each character is processed exactly once. The function takes a bitmask and a character position as input, returns a tuple (is_duplicate, new_bitmask). Algorithm: 1) Create mask = 1 << position. 2) Check if bitmask & mask != 0. If true, return (True, bitmask) - duplicate found. 3) Otherwise, set the bit: new_bitmask = bitmask | mask. 4) Return (False, new_bitmask). Time complexity is O(1) for each character. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 by being the core operation called for each character in the string. Common mistakes include: returning the wrong boolean value (True for duplicate vs False), not preserving the original bitmask when duplicate is found, and incorrect bit shifting. The real pattern: def check_and_set(bitmask, pos): mask = 1 << pos; if bitmask & mask: return True, bitmask; else: return False, bitmask | mask.

examples.trace:
Input: bitmask = 0 (binary: 0), position = 3
Step 1: Create mask = 1 << 3 = 8 (binary: 1000)
Step 2: Check if bitmask & mask != 0: 0 & 8 = 0 → False (no duplicate)
Step 3: Set bit: new_bitmask = 0 | 8 = 8 (binary: 1000)
Step 4: Return (False, 8)

Input: bitmask = 10 (binary: 1010), position = 1
Step 1: Create mask = 1 << 1 = 2 (binary: 10)
Step 2: Check if bitmask & mask != 0: 10 & 2 = 2 (binary: 10) → True (duplicate found)
Step 3: Since duplicate found, return original bitmask unchanged
Step 4: Return (True, 10)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bit Vector Character Tracking
ladders.desc: Use integer bits to track which characters have been seen.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that we can use a single integer as a compact data structure where each bit represents whether we've encountered a specific character. This is efficient because: 1) Checking/setting a bit is O(1), 2) The integer has fixed size (typically 32 or 64 bits), giving O(1) space. The intuition is mapping: character → bit position → bit in integer. For lowercase letters 'a'-'z', we map 'a'→bit 0, 'b'→bit 1, ..., 'z'→bit 25. We need 26 bits, which fits in a standard integer. The algorithm: 1) Initialize tracker = 0 (all bits 0, meaning no characters seen). 2) For each character: calculate position = ord(char) - ord('a'). 3) Check if bit at position is 1 in tracker. 4) If yes, duplicate found. If no, set that bit to 1. Time: O(1) per character check. Space: O(1) for the integer. This connects to L1 by providing the data structure that check_and_set operates on. Common mistakes: incorrect position calculation (off-by-one), not handling non-lowercase characters, integer overflow with many characters. The real pattern: tracker = 0; for ch in s: pos = ord(ch) - ord('a'); if tracker & (1 << pos): return False; tracker |= (1 << pos); return True.

examples.trace:
Start: tracker = 0 (binary: 0)
Character 'a': pos = 0, mask = 1 << 0 = 1 (binary: 1)
Check: 0 & 1 = 0 → not seen
Set: tracker = 0 | 1 = 1 (binary: 1)
Character 'b': pos = 1, mask = 1 << 1 = 2 (binary: 10)
Check: 1 & 2 = 0 → not seen
Set: tracker = 1 | 2 = 3 (binary: 11)
Character 'c': pos = 2, mask = 1 << 2 = 4 (binary: 100)
Check: 3 & 4 = 0 → not seen
Set: tracker = 3 | 4 = 7 (binary: 111)
Final tracker = 7

Start: tracker = 0 (binary: 0)
Character 'x': pos = 23 (since 'x' is 24th letter, 0-indexed: ord('x')-ord('a')=120-97=23)
mask = 1 << 23 = 8388608 (binary: 1 followed by 23 zeros)
Check: 0 & 8388608 = 0 → not seen
Set: tracker = 0 | 8388608 = 8388608
Character 'x': pos = 23, mask = 8388608
Check: 8388608 & 8388608 = 8388608 (non-zero) → DUPLICATE FOUND
Stop processing

testCases.input: Characters: a, b, c, d, e

ladders.title: Building Block — Map Character to Bit Position
ladders.desc: Convert character to corresponding bit position in integer.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is crucial for the bit manipulation approach. We need to establish a consistent mapping from characters to bit positions. The intuition is that characters are represented as numbers (ASCII/Unicode values), and we can transform these to indices within our bit vector. For lowercase English letters, we use: position = ord(character) - ord('a'). This maps 'a'→0, 'b'→1, ..., 'z'→25. Why does this work? Because ASCII values are consecutive: 'a'=97, 'b'=98, ..., 'z'=122. Subtracting 97 ('a') gives us 0-25. The algorithm: 1) Ensure character is lowercase (or handle case). 2) Calculate position = ord(ch) - ord('a'). 3) Verify position is between 0 and 25 (inclusive). Time: O(1). Space: O(1). This connects to L2 by providing the position needed to check/set bits in the tracker. Common mistakes: forgetting to convert to lowercase, incorrect subtraction, not validating input range. The real pattern: def char_to_pos(ch): return ord(ch.lower()) - ord('a').

examples.trace:
Character: 'c'
Step 1: Get ASCII value: ord('c') = 99
Step 2: Get ASCII of 'a': ord('a') = 97
Step 3: Calculate position: 99 - 97 = 2
Verification: 2 is between 0 and 25 ✓
Result: position = 2

Character: 'm'
Step 1: Get ASCII value: ord('m') = 109
Step 2: Get ASCII of 'a': ord('a') = 97
Step 3: Calculate position: 109 - 97 = 12
Verification: 12 is between 0 and 25 ✓
Result: position = 12

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Bitwise AND and OR for Flag Management
ladders.desc: Use bitwise operations to check and set individual bits.
ladders.explanation: These fundamental operations enable efficient bit manipulation. The intuition is that integers are stored in binary, and we can manipulate individual bits using bitwise operators. AND (&) checks if a bit is set: (bitmask & mask) != 0 means the bit is 1. OR (|) sets a bit: bitmask | mask sets the corresponding bit to 1. For example, with bitmask = 5 (binary 101) and mask = 2 (binary 010): AND gives 0 (binary 000) → bit not set; OR gives 7 (binary 111) → bit now set. Algorithm to check bit at position p: 1) Create mask = 1 << p. 2) Check (bitmask & mask) != 0. Algorithm to set bit at position p: bitmask = bitmask | (1 << p). Time: O(1). Space: O(1). This connects to L3 by using these operations once we have the bit position. Common mistakes: using logical AND/OR instead of bitwise, incorrect mask creation, not understanding binary representation. The real pattern: check_bit = (bitmask & (1 << pos)) != 0; set_bit = bitmask | (1 << pos).

examples.trace:
bitmask = 5 (binary: 101)
Position to check: 1
Step 1: Create mask = 1 << 1 = 2 (binary: 010)
Step 2: Compute bitmask & mask = 5 & 2
Binary: 101 & 010 = 000 (decimal 0)
Step 3: Check if result != 0: 0 != 0 is False
Result: Bit at position 1 is 0 (not set)

bitmask = 5 (binary: 101)
Position to set: 1
Step 1: Create mask = 1 << 1 = 2 (binary: 010)
Step 2: Compute bitmask | mask = 5 | 2
Binary: 101 | 010 = 111 (decimal 7)
Result: New bitmask = 7 (binary: 111)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Numbers and Bit Positions
ladders.desc: Understand how integers are represented in binary with bit positions.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept is essential for bit manipulation. The intuition is that computers store integers in binary (base-2) format, where each digit is a bit (0 or 1). A bit's position determines its value: position 0 (rightmost) represents 2^0=1, position 1 represents 2^1=2, position 2 represents 2^2=4, etc. For example, decimal 5 is binary 101: bit at position 0 is 1 (value 1), position 1 is 0 (value 0), position 2 is 1 (value 4). Total = 1+4=5. We can think of each bit as a switch that can be on (1) or off (0). The left shift operator (<<) moves bits left: 1 << 2 means '1' shifted left 2 positions, resulting in binary 100 (decimal 4). This creates a mask with a single 1 at the desired position. Time: O(1) to understand. Space: O(1). This connects to L4 by providing the underlying representation that bitwise operations work on. Common mistakes: confusing bit positions with array indices, misunderstanding binary conversion, not practicing with small numbers. The real pattern: understand that integer N in binary has bits representing powers of 2.

examples.trace:
Decimal: 6
Convert to binary: 6 = 4 + 2 = 2^2 + 2^1
Binary representation: 110 (from left to right: position2=1, position1=1, position0=0)
Breakdown: Position 0 (rightmost): bit = 0, value = 0 × 2^0 = 0
Position 1: bit = 1, value = 1 × 2^1 = 2
Position 2: bit = 1, value = 1 × 2^2 = 4
Total: 0 + 2 + 4 = 6

We want a mask with bit at position 3 set to 1, others 0.
Start with 1 (binary: 1, position 0)
Shift left by 3 positions: 1 << 3
Binary: move '1' three positions left: 1 → 10 → 100 → 1000
Result: binary 1000, which is decimal 8
Verification: 1000 in binary: position3=1 (value 8), positions 2,1,0=0
